title: Find Kth node in inorder traversal
description: Find the Kth node in the inorder traversal of a binary tree

approaches.name: Approach 1: Full Inorder Traversal O(n)

ladders.title: Full Problem — Find Kth Node in Inorder Traversal via Complete Traversal
ladders.desc: Implement a complete inorder traversal to collect all nodes and return the kth element.
ladders.explanation: This approach solves the problem by performing a full inorder traversal of the binary tree, which visits nodes in left-root-right order. The intuition is that inorder traversal naturally produces nodes in sorted order for binary search trees, but for any binary tree, it gives a deterministic sequence. By traversing the entire tree and storing each visited node in a list, we can directly access the kth element from this list. The algorithm starts at the root and recursively traverses the left subtree, processes the current node (adding it to the list), then recursively traverses the right subtree. This ensures all nodes are visited exactly once in inorder order. Time complexity is O(n) where n is the number of nodes, as we visit each node once. Space complexity is O(n) for the list storage plus O(h) for recursion stack (where h is tree height), but O(n) dominates in worst-case skewed trees. This connects to the key subroutine (L1) by implementing that traversal function within a wrapper that handles k indexing. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors with k (e.g., treating k as 0-indexed vs 1-indexed—here we use 1-indexed as per typical problem statements), not handling empty trees or invalid k values, and inefficiently traversing beyond needed nodes. The real code pattern uses recursion with a helper function that accumulates nodes.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Recursive Inorder Traversal to Collect Nodes
ladders.desc: Implement a recursive function that performs inorder traversal and appends nodes to a list.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core of the full solution: a recursive function that traverses a binary tree in inorder order and accumulates node values in a list. The intuition is that recursion naturally mirrors the tree structure: for each node, we first process the left subtree, then the node itself, then the right subtree. This order (left-root-right) defines inorder traversal. The algorithm takes a node and a list as input. If the node is null, return (base case). Otherwise, recursively call on the left child, append the current node's value to the list, then recursively call on the right child. Time complexity is O(n) as it visits each node once. Space complexity is O(n) for the list and O(h) for recursion stack, but O(n) dominates. This feeds into L0 by providing the traversal logic; L0 wraps this to handle k and return the kth element. Common mistakes include forgetting the base case (leading to infinite recursion), modifying the list incorrectly (e.g., using return values instead of in-place updates), and confusion between inorder and other traversals. The real code pattern uses a helper function with list passed by reference.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Inorder Traversal Order (Left-Root-Right)
ladders.desc: Understand and apply the left-root-right sequence for traversing binary trees.
ladders.explanation: The core logic is the inorder traversal order: for each node, visit the left subtree first, then the node itself, then the right subtree. This produces a specific sequence of nodes. The intuition is that this order processes nodes in a depth-first manner, prioritizing left children before parents and right children. For binary search trees, this yields sorted values, but for any binary tree, it gives a deterministic order based on structure. The algorithm can be thought recursively: inorder(node) = inorder(left) + [node] + inorder(right). Time and space are not directly analyzed here as it's a conceptual step, but in implementation, it leads to O(n) time. This feeds into L1 by defining the traversal pattern; L1 implements it recursively. Common mistakes include confusing inorder with preorder (root-left-right) or postorder (left-right-root), and not handling null nodes properly. The real code pattern is embedded in recursive calls.

ladders.title: Building Block — Recursive Tree Traversal
ladders.desc: Use recursion to traverse all nodes in a binary tree.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves using recursion to visit every node in a binary tree. The intuition is that a binary tree has a recursive structure: each node has left and right subtrees that are themselves binary trees. By defining a function that processes a node and recursively calls itself on the left and right children, we can traverse the entire tree. The algorithm: if node is null, return; otherwise, process node (e.g., print value), then recursively call on left child, then on right child for preorder, but order varies. Time complexity is O(n) for n nodes, as each node is visited once. Space complexity is O(h) for recursion stack, where h is tree height. This feeds into L2 by providing the mechanism to implement inorder order; L2 specifies the sequence of recursive calls. Common mistakes include infinite recursion due to missing base case, stack overflow for deep trees, and confusion between tree traversal orders. The real code pattern uses a simple recursive function with null checks.

The intuition is that traversing a tree requires moving from a node to its children, so this operation is fundamental. The algorithm is simple: given a node, check if node.left and node.right exist. Time and space are O(1). This feeds into L3 by enabling recursive calls to subtrees; L3 uses these accesses to traverse further. Common mistakes include assuming children exist without null checks (leading to errors), or confusing node values with child pointers. The real code pattern uses dot notation or getter methods.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Find Kth Node in Inorder Traversal with Recursive Counter
ladders.desc: Complete recursive solution that traverses a binary tree in inorder while counting visited nodes to find the kth node.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires finding the kth node in the inorder traversal of a binary tree without storing the entire traversal. The intuition is that inorder traversal visits nodes in left-root-right order, which for a binary search tree would be sorted order, but here we work with any binary tree. By recursively traversing with a counter, we can stop early when we find the kth node, avoiding unnecessary traversal of the entire tree in best cases. The algorithm uses a helper function that performs inorder traversal: first recursively visit the left subtree, then process the current node by incrementing a counter, and if the counter equals k, store the result and return early to prevent further recursion. Finally, recursively visit the right subtree. The time complexity is O(n) in worst case where we traverse all nodes (e.g., when k equals total nodes or tree is skewed), but can be O(k) in best case if the kth node is found early. Space complexity is O(h) due to recursion stack, where h is the tree height. Connection to L1: This full solution integrates the key subroutine from L1 (the recursive helper) with proper initialization and result handling. Common mistakes include not using a mutable counter (like a list or nonlocal variable) to maintain state across recursive calls, forgetting to return early after finding the kth node (which wastes time), or mishandling k values (e.g., k=0 or k>n). Real code pattern: define a class TreeNode with val, left, right; use a recursive function with parameters node, k, counter, result; traverse left, increment counter at current node, check if counter[0]==k, store node in result[0] and return, then traverse right; initialize counter=[0], result=[None], call helper on root, return result[0].val if result[0] else None.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Recursive Inorder Traversal with Early Termination
ladders.desc: Helper function that recursively traverses a binary tree in inorder, increments a counter at each node, and stops when counter reaches k.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine is the core of the full solution, performing the actual traversal and node counting. The intuition is to simulate inorder traversal recursively while maintaining a global-like counter to track visited nodes; when the counter matches k, we've found the target node and can terminate further recursion to save time. The algorithm: define a function helper(node, k, counter, result) where counter is a mutable list (or nonlocal variable) storing current count, and result is a mutable list to store the kth node. Base case: if node is null, return. First, recursively call helper on node.left. Then, increment counter[0] by 1. If counter[0] == k, set result[0] = node and return immediately to stop. Finally, recursively call helper on node.right. Time complexity for this subroutine alone is O(n) in worst case if we traverse entire tree, but O(k) if we stop early; space is O(h) for recursion stack. Connection to L0: This helper is called from the main function in L0 with proper initialization. Connection to L2: This implements the core logic of counting during traversal. Common mistakes: using an integer counter without mutation (so count doesn't persist across calls), not returning after finding the node (leading to unnecessary right subtree traversal), or incorrect order of operations (must traverse left before processing node). Real code pattern: def helper(node, k, cnt, res): if not node: return; helper(node.left, k, cnt, res); cnt[0] += 1; if cnt[0] == k: res[0] = node; return; helper(node.right, k, cnt, res).

Back to 3: process 3: cnt=[2]; go right to 4, helper(4): go left null, process 4: cnt=[3] (equals k=3), set res[0]=node(4), return. Output node with val 4.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Counting Nodes During Inorder Traversal
ladders.desc: The technique of incrementing a counter each time a node is visited in inorder order to identify the kth position.
ladders.explanation: This core logic involves the idea of associating a count with the inorder traversal sequence. The intuition is that inorder traversal has a defined order (left, node, right), and by counting nodes as we visit them in this order, we can pinpoint the kth node without needing to store the entire traversal. The algorithm: perform a standard inorder traversal (recursively or iteratively), and at the step where you process the current node (i.e., after traversing left subtree and before traversing right), increment a counter. When the counter equals k, you have found the target node. This is efficient because it allows early termination—once the kth node is found, you can stop further traversal. Time complexity is O(k) to O(n) depending on tree structure and k; space is O(h) for recursion. Connection to L1: This logic is implemented in the helper function's processing step. Connection to L3: It relies on understanding inorder traversal order. Common mistakes: counting nodes in preorder or postorder instead of inorder, which gives wrong sequence; or not resetting counter properly between calls. Real code pattern: in a recursive function, after recursive left call, do count += 1; if count == k: return node; then recursive right call.

ladders.title: Building Block — Inorder Traversal of a Binary Tree
ladders.desc: Traversing a binary tree in left-root-right order using recursion.
ladders.explanation: Inorder traversal is a fundamental tree traversal method where you visit the left subtree first, then the current node, then the right subtree. The intuition is that for binary search trees, this yields nodes in sorted order, but for any binary tree, it provides a systematic way to visit all nodes. The algorithm is recursive: define a function inorder(node): if node is null, return; inorder(node.left); process node (e.g., print value); inorder(node.right). This ensures left nodes are visited before their parent, which are visited before right nodes. Time complexity is O(n) since each node is visited once; space is O(h) due to recursion stack. Connection to L2: This traversal order is where we insert the counting logic. Connection to L4: It relies on recursive tree navigation. Common mistakes: confusing inorder with preorder (root-left-right) or postorder (left-right-root), infinite recursion if base case missing, or stack overflow for deep trees. Real code pattern: def inorder(node): if not node: return; inorder(node.left); print(node.val); inorder(node.right).

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Recursive Tree Navigation
ladders.desc: Using recursion to traverse a binary tree by visiting left and right children.
ladders.explanation: This operation involves moving through a binary tree recursively, which is a key skill for tree algorithms. The intuition is that a binary tree is a recursive data structure: each node has up to two children, which are themselves trees. By defining a function that calls itself on the left and right children, we can visit all nodes systematically. The algorithm: for a given node, if it is null, return (base case); otherwise, you can process the node, and recursively call the function on node.left and node.right. This can be adapted for different traversal orders. Time complexity for visiting all nodes is O(n); space is O(h) for recursion depth. Connection to L3: This is how inorder traversal is implemented. Connection to L5: It builds on understanding recursion and tree structure. Common mistakes: not handling null nodes (leading to errors), infinite recursion if tree has cycles (not in standard binary trees), or incorrect order of recursive calls. Real code pattern: def traverse(node): if node is None: return; # process node here; traverse(node.left); traverse(node.right).

ladders.desc: Understanding a binary tree node and the basic recursive function with a null check.
ladders.explanation: This foundation covers the simplest elements needed for tree algorithms: what a binary tree node is and how to write a recursive function that handles the base case of a null node. A binary tree node typically has a value and pointers to left and right children. In code, it's often defined as a class with attributes val, left, right. Recursion is a technique where a function calls itself; for trees, we use recursion to traverse children. The base case is when the node is null (i.e., no child), which stops the recursion to prevent infinite loops. The intuition: trees are recursive by nature—each node can be seen as the root of a subtree. By checking if node is null, we ensure we only process actual nodes. Time/space: a single recursive call is O(1) time and space, but sets up for larger traversals. Connection to L4: This is the building block for navigating trees recursively. Common mistakes: forgetting the null check (causing attribute errors on None), or misunderstanding node structure. Real code pattern: class TreeNode: def __init__(self, val=0, left=None, right=None): self.val = val; self.left = left; self.right = right. Then, def basic_recursion(node): if node is None: return; # do something; basic_recursion(node.left); basic_recursion(node.right).

approaches.name: Approach 3: Iterative Inorder Traversal with Stack O(n)
approaches.summary: Use an iterative inorder traversal with a stack to simulate recursion, decrementing k as nodes are visited until k reaches zero. This provides explicit control over traversal and can handle large trees without recursion overhead.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(h) where h is tree height (due to stack usage)

ladders.title: Full Problem — Find Kth Node in Inorder Traversal Using Iterative Stack
ladders.desc: Complete iterative solution using stack to find Kth node in inorder traversal
ladders.explanation:
This is the complete solution to find the Kth node in the inorder traversal of a binary tree using an iterative approach with a stack. The intuition is to simulate the recursive inorder traversal process explicitly using a stack data structure, which gives us fine-grained control over the traversal order. We maintain a current node pointer and a stack to track nodes we need to revisit. The algorithm starts by pushing all leftmost nodes onto the stack (simulating the left subtree traversal). Then we pop nodes one by one, decrementing k each time we process a node. When k reaches 0, we've found our target node. If the popped node has a right child, we push all leftmost nodes of that right subtree onto the stack (simulating the right subtree traversal). This approach avoids recursion overhead and provides O(n) time complexity with O(h) space complexity where h is the tree height.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Initialize stack as empty list and current = root
2. While stack is not empty OR current is not null:
   a. While current is not null:
      - Push current onto stack
      - Move current to current.left
   b. Pop node from stack
   c. Decrement k by 1
   d. If k == 0, return node.value
   e. Set current = node.right
3. Return null (if k > number of nodes)

Time complexity: O(n) in worst case where we might traverse all nodes
Space complexity: O(h) where h is tree height, worst case O(n) for skewed trees

Connection to L1: This level combines the iterative traversal pattern from L1 with the k-tracking logic to solve the complete problem.

Common mistakes:
1. Forgetting to handle the case when k > number of nodes
2. Not properly updating the current pointer after processing a node
3. Confusing when to push nodes vs when to pop them
4. Off-by-one errors with k decrementing

Real code pattern:
def kth_node_iterative(root, k):
    stack = []
    current = root
    
    while stack or current:
        while current:
            stack.append(current)
            current = current.left
        
        node = stack.pop()
        k -= 1
        if k == 0:
            return node.val
        
        current = node.right
    
    return None

Step 4: Pop 3: k=2, current=4
Step 5: Push 4: stack=[5,4], current=null
Step 6: Pop 4: k=1, current=null
Step 7: Pop 5: k=0 → Found! Return 5

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Iterative Inorder Traversal with Stack
ladders.desc: Implement iterative inorder traversal using stack without k tracking
ladders.explanation:
This sub-routine implements the core iterative inorder traversal pattern using a stack, which is the foundation for the complete solution. The intuition is to simulate the recursive call stack explicitly. In recursive inorder traversal, we first traverse the left subtree, then process the current node, then traverse the right subtree. With a stack, we can achieve the same order by pushing nodes as we go left, then popping to process them, then going right.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Initialize empty stack and set current = root
2. While stack is not empty OR current is not null:
   a. While current is not null:
      - Push current onto stack
      - Set current = current.left
   b. Pop node from stack
   c. Process node (print/store value)
   d. Set current = node.right

Time complexity: O(n) where n is number of nodes
Space complexity: O(h) where h is tree height

Connection to L0: This traversal pattern becomes the backbone of the complete solution when we add k tracking at the processing step.

Common mistakes:
1. Infinite loop when not properly updating current pointer
2. Forgetting to check both stack and current in while condition
3. Processing nodes in wrong order
4. Not handling null nodes correctly

Real code pattern:
def iterative_inorder(root):
    stack = []
    current = root
    result = []
    
    while stack or current:
        while current:
            stack.append(current)
            current = current.left
        
        node = stack.pop()
        result.append(node.val)
        
        current = node.right
    
    return result

ladders.title: Core Logic — Simulating Recursion with Explicit Stack
ladders.desc: Understand how to use stack to simulate recursive traversal order
ladders.explanation:
This level focuses on the core insight of using a stack to simulate recursive function calls. The intuition is that recursion uses an implicit call stack, and we can recreate this behavior explicitly with our own stack data structure. For inorder traversal specifically, the recursive pattern is: traverse left, process node, traverse right. With a stack, we achieve this by: 1) pushing nodes as we go left (simulating recursive calls), 2) popping to process nodes (simulating returning from recursion), and 3) going right after processing (simulating the next recursive call).

The key insight is that the stack preserves the order of nodes we need to revisit. When we go left, we push the current node because we'll need to come back to it after processing its left subtree. This mimics how recursive calls remember where to return.

Step-by-step mental model:
1. Start at root, push all left children onto stack
2. When can't go left anymore, pop from stack (this is the next node in inorder)
3. After processing popped node, go to its right child
4. Repeat from step 1 with the right child as new starting point

Time complexity analysis: Each node is pushed and popped exactly once → O(n)
Space complexity: Maximum depth of stack equals tree height → O(h)

Connection to L1: This understanding enables implementing the complete iterative traversal pattern.

Common mistakes:
1. Not understanding why we need to push nodes before going left
2. Confusing this with other traversal orders (preorder/postorder)
3. Not recognizing this as simulation of recursion

Real code pattern:
# The pattern is:
# 1. Go left as far as possible, pushing nodes
# 2. Pop and process
# 3. Go right
# Repeat until done

ladders.title: Building Block — Binary Tree Traversal Orders
ladders.desc: Understand inorder traversal and how it differs from preorder/postorder
ladders.explanation:
This building block establishes the fundamental concept of binary tree traversal orders, with special focus on inorder traversal. Inorder traversal visits nodes in the order: left subtree, current node, right subtree. For binary search trees, this yields nodes in ascending order. The intuition is that we completely process the left side before the current node, then move to the right side.

Comparison of traversal orders:
1. Preorder: Current node, left subtree, right subtree (root first)
2. Inorder: Left subtree, current node, right subtree (sorted order for BST)
3. Postorder: Left subtree, right subtree, current node (root last)

For inorder specifically:
- Visit all nodes in left subtree recursively
- Visit current node
- Visit all nodes in right subtree recursively

This order is natural for many operations:
- Getting sorted output from BST
- Expression tree evaluation (infix notation)
- Finding kth smallest element

Step-by-step example: For tree with root=5, left=3, right=7:
1. Go to left subtree (3)
2. 3 has no left, so visit 3
3. 3 has no right, so return to 5
4. Visit 5
5. Go to right subtree (7)
6. Visit 7
Result: 3,5,7

Time complexity: O(n) for all traversals
Space complexity: O(h) for recursive implementation

Connection to L2: Understanding inorder traversal is prerequisite for simulating it with a stack.

Common mistakes:
1. Confusing inorder with other traversal orders
2. Not understanding recursion depth
3. Forgetting that traversal visits every node exactly once

Real code pattern:
def inorder_recursive(node):
    if node is None:
        return
    inorder_recursive(node.left)
    print(node.val)  # Process current node
    inorder_recursive(node.right)

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Stack Push/Pop for Tree Navigation
ladders.desc: Use stack operations to navigate and backtrack in a tree
ladders.explanation:
This level focuses on the fundamental operation of using a stack's push and pop operations to navigate a tree structure with the ability to backtrack. The intuition is that a stack's LIFO (Last-In-First-Out) property perfectly matches the need to remember where we came from in tree traversal. When we go deeper into the tree (e.g., going left), we push the current node onto the stack to remember we need to come back to it later. When we reach a dead end (null node), we pop from the stack to backtrack to the most recent node we need to process.

Key operations:
1. Push: Remember a node to process later (when we go deeper)
2. Pop: Retrieve the most recently remembered node (when we need to backtrack)
3. Peek: Look at top without removing (optional)

For tree navigation specifically:
- Push when moving to a child node (to remember parent)
- Pop when done with a subtree (to return to parent)
- The stack essentially stores the "path" from root to current node

Example: Navigating root→left child→left grandchild:
1. Push root (stack: [root])
2. Go to left child, push it (stack: [root, left])
3. Go to left grandchild, push it (stack: [root, left, leftGrand])
4. Hit null, pop leftGrand (backtrack)
5. Process leftGrand
6. Pop left (backtrack)
7. Process left
8. etc.

Time complexity: Each push/pop is O(1)
Space complexity: Stack grows with depth of navigation

Connection to L3: Stack operations enable implementing the traversal orders conceptually understood at L3.

Common mistakes:
1. Pushing when should pop or vice versa
2. Not checking if stack is empty before popping
3. Forgetting to push the current node before moving to child
4. Confusing stack order (LIFO) with queue order (FIFO)

Real code pattern:
stack = []
current = root

# Going left/down:
while current:
    stack.append(current)  # Push to remember
    current = current.left

# Backtracking/up:
if stack:
    node = stack.pop()  # Get most recent
    current = node.right  # Go right

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Binary Tree Node Structure and Navigation
ladders.desc: Understand binary tree nodes, pointers, and basic navigation
ladders.explanation:
This is the foundational concept of binary tree structure and basic navigation. A binary tree is a hierarchical data structure where each node has at most two children: left and right. Each node typically contains:
1. Value/data stored in the node
2. Pointer/reference to left child (or null)
3. Pointer/reference to right child (or null)

The intuition is that trees organize data hierarchically, with parent-child relationships. Navigation means moving from one node to another using these pointers.

Basic navigation operations:
1. Go to left child: current = current.left
2. Go to right child: current = current.right
3. Check if node exists: if current is not null
4. Check if leaf: if current.left is null and current.right is null

Tree terminology:
- Root: Topmost node (no parent)
- Leaf: Node with no children
- Internal node: Node with at least one child
- Parent/Child: Relationship between connected nodes
- Subtree: A node and all its descendants

Example: Simple tree with root=5, left=3, right=7:
- Node 5 has value 5, left pointer to node 3, right pointer to node 7
- Node 3 has value 3, left=null, right=null (leaf)
- Node 7 has value 7, left=null, right=null (leaf)

To navigate:
1. Start at root (node 5)
2. To go left: follow left pointer to node 3
3. To go back: need to remember where we came from (introduces need for stack)
4. To go right from root: follow right pointer to node 7

Time complexity: Navigation to child is O(1)
Space complexity: Storing tree requires O(n) space

Connection to L4: Understanding node structure and navigation is prerequisite for using stacks to manage navigation.

Common mistakes:
1. Accessing left/right of null node (null pointer exception)
2. Not checking if child exists before navigating
3. Confusing node value with node reference
4. Not understanding tree is not linear like array

Real code pattern:
class TreeNode:
    def __init__(self, val):
        self.val = val
        self.left = None
        self.right = None

# Navigation:
current = root
if current.left:  # Check exists
    current = current.left  # Go left
if current.right:  # Check exists
    current = current.right  # Go right

approaches.name: Approach 4: Augmented Tree with Subtree Sizes O(h)
approaches.summary: Augment each node with the size of its subtree, then use this information to navigate directly to the kth node by comparing k with left subtree sizes. This optimizes for repeated queries but requires preprocessing.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(h) where h is tree height (for a single query after preprocessing)

ladders.title: Full Problem — Find Kth Node in Inorder Traversal Using Augmented Tree
ladders.desc: Complete solution using subtree size augmentation to find kth node in O(h) time after preprocessing.
ladders.explanation: This approach solves the problem of finding the kth node in an inorder traversal efficiently for repeated queries by augmenting each node with the size of its subtree. The intuition is that by knowing how many nodes are in each subtree, we can navigate directly to the kth node without traversing the entire tree. After preprocessing to compute subtree sizes (which takes O(n) time), each query can be answered in O(h) time by comparing k with the left subtree size at each node. If k is less than or equal to the left subtree size, the kth node is in the left subtree. If k equals left subtree size plus 1, the current node is the kth node. Otherwise, we adjust k and move to the right subtree. This is particularly useful when multiple queries are needed on the same tree, as the preprocessing cost is amortized. The step-by-step algorithm involves two phases: first, a postorder traversal to compute subtree sizes and store them in each node; second, a search function that uses these sizes to find the kth node. Time complexity is O(n) for preprocessing and O(h) per query, where h is the tree height. Space complexity is O(n) for storing subtree sizes. Connection to L1: This level integrates the size computation and search functions into a complete solution. Common mistakes include not handling 1-based indexing correctly (k should be 1-indexed), forgetting to update k when moving to the right subtree, and not checking for null nodes. Real code pattern in Python-style pseudocode: class TreeNode with val, left, right, and size attributes; compute_size function for postorder traversal; find_kth function that navigates using sizes. Ensure k is valid (1 ≤ k ≤ total nodes).

examples.input: Tree: [1,2,3,4,5,null,7] (level-order), k=4
examples.trace: Step 1: Build tree with nodes 1 (root), 2 (left child of 1), 3 (right child of 1), 4 (left child of 2), 5 (right child of 2), null (left child of 3), 7 (right child of 3). Step 2: Compute subtree sizes via postorder traversal: Node 4: size=1 (left=null, right=null). Node 5: size=1. Node 2: size=1+1+1=3 (left size=1 from node 4, right size=1 from node 5, plus itself). Node 7: size=1. Node 3: size=0+1+1=2 (left=null, right size=1 from node 7, plus itself). Node 1: size=3+2+1=6 (left size=3 from node 2, right size=2 from node 3, plus itself). Step 3: Find kth node with k=4. Start at root node 1 with size=6, left size=3. Since k=4 > left size=3, adjust k = k - left size - 1 = 4 - 3 - 1 = 0, move to right subtree (node 3). At node 3 with size=2, left size=0. Since k=0 <= left size=0, move to left subtree (null), but k=0 indicates we overshot; actually, since k was adjusted to 0, we need to check: when k=0, it means the current node after adjustment is the target, but here we moved to right. Re-evaluate: At node 1, k=4, left size=3. Since k > left size, we subtract and move right. At node 3, new k=4-3-1=0? Wait, correct adjustment: k = k - left_size - 1 = 4 - 3 - 1 = 0. This means the target is the current node (node 1) if k=0? No, standard logic: if k == left_size + 1, current node is answer. Here, at node 1, k=4, left_size=3, so k == left_size+1? 4 == 3+1=4, yes! So node 1 is the 4th node. Let's verify inorder: [4,2,5,1,3,7] -> 4th element is 1. So output is node 1. Correction in trace: At node 1, k=4, left_size=3, since k == left_size+1=4, return node 1. No need to move. Output: node with value 1.

examples.input: Tree: [10,5,15,3,7,null,18] (level-order), k=5
examples.trace: Step 1: Build tree: root 10, left 5, right 15, left of 5 is 3, right of 5 is 7, left of 15 is null, right of 15 is 18. Step 2: Compute subtree sizes postorder: Node 3: size=1. Node 7: size=1. Node 5: size=1+1+1=3. Node 18: size=1. Node 15: size=0+1+1=2. Node 10: size=3+2+1=6. Step 3: Find k=5. Start at root 10, left_size=3. Since k=5 > left_size=3, adjust k = k - left_size - 1 = 5 - 3 - 1 = 1, move to right subtree (node 15). At node 15, left_size=0. Since k=1 > left_size=0, but check: k == left_size+1? 1 == 0+1=1, yes, so current node 15 is the answer. Inorder traversal: [3,5,7,10,15,18] -> 5th element is 15. Output: node with value 15.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Navigate to Kth Node Using Subtree Sizes
ladders.desc: Helper function that uses augmented subtree sizes to find the kth node in O(h) time.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core search function that leverages precomputed subtree sizes to efficiently locate the kth node in an inorder traversal. The intuition is that by storing the number of nodes in each subtree, we can make informed decisions at each node without traversing unnecessary parts of the tree. At any node, the inorder traversal visits all nodes in the left subtree first, then the current node, then the right subtree. Therefore, if k is less than or equal to the size of the left subtree, the kth node lies in the left subtree. If k equals the left subtree size plus 1, the current node is the kth node. Otherwise, the kth node is in the right subtree, and we adjust k by subtracting the left subtree size and the current node (i.e., k = k - left_size - 1) before recursing into the right subtree. This reduces the problem size at each step, leading to O(h) time complexity. The step-by-step algorithm: define a function find_kth(node, k) that returns the kth node. If node is null, return null. Get left_size from node.left.size (or 0 if left is null). If k <= left_size, recurse on left subtree with same k. If k == left_size + 1, return current node. Else, recurse on right subtree with k adjusted as k - left_size - 1. Time complexity is O(h) where h is tree height, as we traverse at most one path from root to leaf. Space complexity is O(h) for recursion stack, or O(1) if iterative. Connection to L0: This function is used in the full solution after preprocessing. Connection to L2: This implements the core logic of comparing k with subtree sizes. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in k adjustment, not handling null nodes properly, and assuming k is 0-indexed (it should be 1-indexed). Real code pattern: def find_kth(node, k): if not node: return None; left_size = node.left.size if node.left else 0; if k <= left_size: return find_kth(node.left, k); elif k == left_size + 1: return node; else: return find_kth(node.right, k - left_size - 1). Ensure k is valid (1 ≤ k ≤ total nodes).

examples.input: Node with size=6 (left size=3, right size=2), k=4
examples.trace: Step 1: Start at node with left_size=3. Step 2: Check k=4. Since k=4 > left_size=3, but compare: k == left_size+1? 4 == 3+1=4, yes. Step 3: Therefore, return current node. No recursion needed.

examples.input: Node with size=5 (left size=2, right size=2), k=5
examples.output: Result from right subtree with adjusted k=2
examples.trace: Step 1: Start at node with left_size=2. Step 2: Check k=5. Since k=5 > left_size=2, and k != left_size+1 (5 != 3), so adjust k = k - left_size - 1 = 5 - 2 - 1 = 2. Step 3: Recurse to right subtree with k=2. Step 4: In right subtree, proceed similarly based on its sizes.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Compare K with Left Subtree Size to Determine Search Direction
ladders.desc: Central insight: use left subtree size to decide if kth node is in left, current, or right subtree.
ladders.explanation: This core logic is the pivotal idea that enables efficient navigation in the augmented tree approach. The intuition stems from the properties of inorder traversal: for any node, the traversal order is left subtree nodes, then the node itself, then right subtree nodes. Therefore, the position of the kth node relative to the current node can be determined by comparing k with the number of nodes in the left subtree. Specifically, let left_size be the size of the left subtree. If k is less than or equal to left_size, the kth node must be in the left subtree because the first left_size nodes in inorder are all from the left subtree. If k equals left_size + 1, the current node is the kth node, as it comes immediately after the left subtree nodes. If k is greater than left_size + 1, the kth node is in the right subtree, and its position within the right subtree is adjusted by subtracting the nodes already counted (left_size nodes plus the current node). This logic reduces the search space at each step, allowing us to descend the tree in O(h) time. The step-by-step process: at each node, compute left_size (from augmented data). Compare k with left_size. Based on the comparison, either recurse left, return current, or recurse right with updated k. This is a divide-and-conquer strategy that prunes branches. Time complexity per node is O(1), leading to O(h) overall. Space complexity is O(h) for recursion. Connection to L1: This logic is implemented in the find_kth function. Connection to L3: It relies on having accurate subtree sizes. Common mistakes include miscalculating left_size (e.g., forgetting to handle null left child), using 0-indexed k (should be 1-indexed), and not updating k correctly when moving right. Real code pattern: if k <= left_size: search left; elif k == left_size + 1: return node; else: search right with k - left_size - 1. This ensures efficient traversal.

examples.input: left_size=3, k=2
examples.trace: Step 1: Given left_size=3, k=2. Step 2: Compare k and left_size: k=2 <= left_size=3. Step 3: Therefore, the kth node is in the left subtree. Step 4: Proceed to search left subtree with the same k=2.

examples.input: left_size=0, k=1
examples.output: Current node is the kth node
examples.trace: Step 1: Given left_size=0, k=1. Step 2: Compare k and left_size: k=1 > left_size=0, but check k == left_size+1? 1 == 0+1=1, yes. Step 3: Therefore, the current node is the 1st node in inorder. Step 4: Return current node.

ladders.title: Building Block — Compute Subtree Sizes via Postorder Traversal
ladders.desc: Augment each node with the size of its subtree using a postorder traversal.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves preprocessing the binary tree to store the size of each node's subtree, which is essential for the efficient search in the augmented tree approach. The intuition is that by traversing the tree in postorder (left, right, root), we can compute the size of a node's subtree after we know the sizes of its children. The size of a node is defined as the total number of nodes in its subtree, including itself. For a leaf node, the size is 1. For an internal node, the size is the sum of the sizes of its left and right children plus 1. This computation ensures that each node's size accurately reflects the number of nodes that will be visited in its subtree during an inorder traversal. The step-by-step algorithm: define a function compute_size(node) that returns the size of the subtree rooted at node. If node is null, return 0. Recursively compute size of left subtree by calling compute_size(node.left). Recursively compute size of right subtree by calling compute_size(node.right). Set node.size = left_size + right_size + 1. Return node.size. This traversal visits each node exactly once, so time complexity is O(n). Space complexity is O(h) for recursion stack, where h is tree height. The computed sizes are stored in each node, using O(n) extra space overall. Connection to L2: These sizes are used in the core logic to compare with k. Connection to L4: This relies on basic tree traversal and node attribute updates. Common mistakes include forgetting to handle null nodes (return 0), not storing the size in the node, and using preorder or inorder traversal which wouldn't work because child sizes must be known before parent. Real code pattern: def compute_size(node): if not node: return 0; left_size = compute_size(node.left); right_size = compute_size(node.right); node.size = left_size + right_size + 1; return node.size. This ensures all nodes are augmented correctly.

examples.input: Tree: [1,2,3] (root 1, left 2, right 3)
examples.trace: Step 1: Start at root 1, but postorder traverses left first. Step 2: Go to node 2. Since 2 has no children, left_size=0, right_size=0, so 2.size = 0+0+1=1, return 1. Step 3: Go to node 3. Similarly, 3.size = 0+0+1=1, return 1. Step 4: Back to node 1. left_size=1 (from node 2), right_size=1 (from node 3), so 1.size = 1+1+1=3, return 3. Step 5: All sizes computed.

examples.input: Tree: [1,2,null,3] (root 1, left 2, left of 2 is 3)
examples.trace: Step 1: Postorder traversal: start at node 3. 3 has no children, so 3.size=1, return 1. Step 2: Move to node 2. left_size=1 (from node 3), right_size=0 (null), so 2.size = 1+0+1=2, return 2. Step 3: Move to node 1. left_size=2 (from node 2), right_size=0 (null), so 1.size = 2+0+1=3, return 3. Step 4: Sizes computed.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Tree Traversal and Node Attribute Update
ladders.desc: Fundamental operations: traverse a binary tree and update node attributes (like size).
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves two key skills: traversing a binary tree and modifying node attributes during traversal. Tree traversal is the process of visiting each node in the tree exactly once in a specific order. For computing subtree sizes, postorder traversal is used because we need to process children before the parent. Updating node attributes means storing additional information in each node, such as a size field, which can be done by setting node.size = value. The intuition is that by combining traversal with attribute updates, we can propagate information from leaves to the root. The step-by-step process: define a recursive function that visits a node. First, recursively traverse the left subtree. Then, recursively traverse the right subtree. After both children are processed, compute the node's size as the sum of children's sizes plus 1, and store it in node.size. This ensures that when we return to a parent node, the sizes of its children are already computed. Time complexity is O(n) as each node is visited once. Space complexity is O(h) for recursion stack. Connection to L3: This operation is used to compute subtree sizes. Connection to L5: It builds on understanding tree structure and recursion. Common mistakes include traversing in the wrong order (e.g., preorder would compute sizes incorrectly), not handling null nodes, and forgetting to update the attribute. Real code pattern: def traverse(node): if not node: return; traverse(node.left); traverse(node.right); node.size = compute_size_based_on_children(). For size computation specifically: left_size = node.left.size if node.left else 0; right_size similarly; node.size = left_size + right_size + 1. This pattern is fundamental for many tree augmentation problems.

examples.input: Tree: [1,2] (root 1, left 2), update sizes
examples.trace: Step 1: Start at root 1. Since postorder, first go to left child 2. Step 2: At node 2, it has no children, so compute size: left_size=0, right_size=0, so 2.size = 0+0+1=1. Step 3: Return to node 1. Now compute its size: left_size=1 (from node 2), right_size=0 (null), so 1.size = 1+0+1=2. Step 4: Both nodes updated.

examples.input: Tree: [1,null,2] (root 1, right 2), update sizes
examples.trace: Step 1: Start at root 1. Postorder: first left subtree is null, so return 0. Step 2: Then go to right child 2. At node 2, no children, so 2.size = 1. Step 3: Return to node 1. Compute size: left_size=0, right_size=1, so 1.size = 0+1+1=2. Step 4: Nodes updated.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Binary Tree Structure and Inorder Traversal
ladders.desc: Prerequisite: understand binary tree nodes and inorder traversal order.
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept covers the basics of binary trees and inorder traversal, which are essential for understanding the problem. A binary tree is a hierarchical data structure where each node has at most two children: left and right. Each node typically contains a value and references to its children. Inorder traversal is a method to visit all nodes in a specific order: first traverse the left subtree, then visit the current node, then traverse the right subtree. This results in a sequence that, for binary search trees, gives nodes in sorted order, but for general binary trees, it simply reflects this left-root-right pattern. The intuition is that by following this order, we can list nodes in a way that respects the tree structure. For example, in a tree with root A, left child B, and right child C, inorder traversal visits B, then A, then C. This order is crucial because the problem asks for the kth node in this sequence. Understanding this traversal helps in grasping why comparing k with left subtree size works: the left subtree nodes come first in inorder. Step-by-step: to perform inorder traversal recursively, define a function inorder(node) that if node is not null, calls inorder(node.left), processes node (e.g., prints value), then calls inorder(node.right). Time complexity is O(n) as each node is visited once. Space complexity is O(h) for recursion stack. Connection to L4: This traversal is the basis for more complex operations like computing sizes. Common mistakes include confusing inorder with preorder or postorder, not handling null nodes, and misunderstanding the order for nested subtrees. Real code pattern: def inorder(node): if node: inorder(node.left); print(node.val); inorder(node.right). This foundational knowledge is key for any tree-related algorithm.

examples.input: Tree: [1,2,3] (root 1, left 2, right 3)
examples.trace: Step 1: Start at root 1. Since inorder, first go to left child 2. Step 2: At node 2, it has no children, so process node 2 (add to sequence). Step 3: Return to node 1, process node 1. Step 4: Go to right child 3, process node 3. Step 5: Sequence is 2,1,3.

examples.input: Tree: [1,2,null,3] (root 1, left 2, left of 2 is 3)
examples.trace: Step 1: Start at root 1. Go to left child 2. Step 2: At node 2, go to its left child 3. Step 3: At node 3, no children, process 3. Step 4: Return to node 2, process 2. Step 5: Return to node 1, process 1. Step 6: Sequence is 3,2,1.
